methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Seed → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.5x) to $4M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Robotics & Manufacturing (found 0 same-stage, 0 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 7 same-sector peers exist in Robotics & Manufacturing, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

data.narrative:
Grain Weevil is a seed-stage robotics and manufacturing company founded by Chad Johnson and Ben Johnson that has raised $3.5M to date. The robotics and manufacturing sector comprises nine peer companies—one public (277810.KQ) and eight private entities including Divergent, Exotec, Locus Robotics, Bedrock Robotics, and Dexterity—though meaningful valuation comparables at the same stage remain sparse.

The valuation analysis relies on stage-progression methodology, which is applicable given the company's seed stage and capital raised. Using total raised of $3.5M as a proxy base value and applying a 2.5x step-up multiplier typical for progression from seed to the next stage yields an implied valuation of $8.75M. Peer-median analysis was not applicable; while seven robotics and manufacturing peers exist in the dataset, none met the threshold of three or more comparable companies at the same stage or adjacent stages with similar valuation tiers. Deploying an all-sector median would introduce unjustifiable noise given the specificity required for early-stage valuation.

The triangulated range supported by applicable methodology is $7M (low) to $10M (high), with a median point of $9M. This single-method conclusion carries elevated uncertainty stemming from the limited comparable set at seed stage in this sector. Key variables affecting valuation include operational progress post-funding, product validation milestones, and capital efficiency relative to sector benchmarks—all factors that remain outside the quantitative data provided.
